@Saylenty Thanks for the PR :)  The code has a long history starting from the original project https://code.google.com/p/dpkt/. There is obviously lots of room for cleanup, in general we're trying to balance that cleanup with avoiding changes that might negatively impact existing users. In particular the @property changes are great (thumbs up) but if an outside script is calling get_opcode() for instance and that method is now gone than we might upset some folks. So the PR is great but we might want to include any old getters and setters with a small wrapper that issues a DeprecationWarning and then calls the new code underneath. We'll do that for a month or two and then remove them. Sound reasonable?

Perhaps the simplest fix would be to offer a top level `six.getstatusoutput` function?

The main downside I can see to that is that it would lead to the following odd discrepancy:

    from six.moves import getoutput
    from six import getstatusoutput

Since it's only one function, `getoutput` could also be made top-level, rather than having one of them top-level and the other under `moves`.

Here is the coverage histogram:
![contigs_coveragebwa_sxpx bam](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/5191161/16975675/5731ab1e-4e0e-11e6-83a7-68a78c9778aa.png)

The assembly was made with [FALCON](https://github.com/PacificBiosciences/FALCON) using only PacBio sequencing data. I'm using BESST to scaffold it with the Illumina libs I've got.

I've done this before on a different PacBio assembly, without issue. Here's the coverage histogram from that run:
![contigs_coveragesxpx_pb_denovo_bwa bam](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/5191161/16975758/3041c51a-4e0f-11e6-9c03-0e1793ae5132.png)

And the resulting gap predictions:

![gappredictions](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/5191161/16975777/45dd7cde-4e0f-11e6-88ec-ff1d875213e2.png)


I'm thinking the coverage data is just too skewed in the new assembly, so that essentially everything is considered an outlier?

Maybe I can manually specify the coverage cutoff with `-z`? I'm curious to try setting `-z_min 1` to get rid of everything with 0 coverage. However, something I noticed a while ago is that removing low-coverage sequences actually decreased scaffold quality substantially. But that was dealing with DBG assembly of Illumina data.

@mschatz (and @hanfang if you're still around, sorry that this slipped through the cracks):

Thanks for the example. I can reproduce the error using a subset of the GENCODE v19 GTF (chr11).

```bash
wget -O- \
  ftp://ftp.ebi.ac.uk/pub/databases/gencode/Gencode_human/release_19/gencode.v19.annotation.gtf \
  | zcat \
  | grep "^chr11" > chr11.gtf
```

However, your example asks for a BED12 line for an entire *gene* with possibly many transcripts. If I change `"gene_id"` in your example to `"transcript_id"`, it works as expected.

So, short answer: use `"transcript_id"`, not `"gene_id"`.

I actually don't know what the expected behavior should be for a BED12 line representing a gene with multiple transcripts. To investigate, here's an ASCII-art gene:

```
123456789012345
=====------====
  ===------====
```

The corresponding BED12 line -- with overlapping exons and the last exon duplicated -- is this:

```
chr1  1  15  .  0  +  1  15  0,0,0  4 4,5,3,3  0,2,11,11
```

Surprisingly to me, this actually worked as a custom track in UCSC. The Genome Browser displays the feature as a single transcript, sort of merging together the overlapping exons:

```
123456789012345
=====------====
  ===------====

=====------====  # UCSC visually represents it like this
```

The error you're seeing is because I've been assuming one BED12 line corresponds to one transcript with non-overlapping exons (this was pointed out in the [docstring](https://github.com/daler/gffutils/blob/v0.9/gffutils/interface.py#L1150), but could be a stronger warning). Specifically, your example code will cause the error on genes with multiple transcripts in which the the 3'-most exon of at least two transcripts are nested:

```
===---======
===-----==
```

I hesitate to rewrite the `bed12()` method to handle overlapping exons aggregated at the gene level because I feel like that's asking for downstream problems. While it looks like UCSC will display them correctly, downstream tools consuming the BED12 would need to handle this carefully (for example, it could get tricky to avoid double-counting the last exon in the first example above).

If the goal is to use gene-level BED12 lines with overlapping exons, would you describe how you're using them to help guide what the fix should be here?

Hi Bertoky,

Thanks for the feedback. I think I have found the problem.
If I change line 32 to:
```python
eaf.add_ref_annotation(tier, tier_ref, tuplanueva[0]+1, tuplanueva[3])
```
It works. This is because two annotations match the original time point `tuplanueva[0]`. When multiple annotations match the timepoint the first one gets chosen. A way to circumvent this is to add 1ms to the timepoint or take the middle. I'll make a note in the documentation about this in the next release version.

Thanks again for the feedback!

Best,
Mart

ps. The safe and clean way would be then to change line 32 to:
```python
eaf.add_ref_annotation(tier, tier_ref, (tuplanueva[0]+tuplanueva[1])/2, tuplanueva[3])
```

Multiprocessing to load tdms files using npTDMS works well. 

Use the following code as a guide... 

from multiprocessing import Pool
from nptdms import TdmsFile
fileList = [ 
    "probe01.tdms",
    "probe02.tdms",
    .
    .
    .
    "probe24.tdms"
]

def tdmsProcess(fileName):
  print("tdmsProcess for ", fileName," started")
  tdmsObject = TdmsFile(fileName, memmap_dir="./tmp")
  print("tdmsProcess for ", fileName, " complete")
  return tdmsObject, fileName

pool = Pool()
tdmsFiles = pool.map(tdmsProcess, fileList)
pool.close()
pool.join()

tdms object is a list containing the desired npTDMS objects.  Depending on your python you may need to use a pool function .get() of some sort to retrieve value returned from pool.map() - it works for me as is in python3 though.  Watch out... if your tdms files are too large, you will run out of memory at some point.

I came across the following articles which I thought you might find interesting. They outline why a project may prefer a "tests outside application code" approach.

- https://docs.pytest.org/en/latest/goodpractices.html#tests-outside-application-code
- https://blog.ionelmc.ro/2014/05/25/python-packaging/#the-structure

The end of the 2nd article has the summary:

> You'll notice that I don't include the tests in the installed packages. Because:
>
> Module discovery tools will trip over your test modules. Strange things usually happen in test module. The help builtin does module discovery. E.g.:
>
> ```
>    >>> help('modules')
>    Please wait a moment while I gather a list of all available modules...
>
>    __future__          antigravity         html                select
>    ...
> ```
>
> Tests usually require additional dependencies to run, so they aren't useful by their own - you can't run them directly.
>
> Tests are concerned with development, not usage.
>
> It's extremely unlikely that the user of the library will run the tests instead of the library's developer. E.g.: you don't run the tests for Django while testing your apps - Django is already tested.
So I assume we can close this :-)

Currently `__eq__` is only defined for Primitive values (string, int, bool, etc), not those derived from Sequence or Set. The exception to this is some of the `x509` sequences have explicit comparisons defined in the RFCs, so those have been implemented.

The current implementation for primitive values factors in a number of things. You've found one of the edge cases, that is where BER and DER encodings of the same value will not compare equally.

I don't know if it is a good idea to provide a general case comparison for structured types, since that may be defined differently based on the use case. I suppose that if the DER encodings are exactly the same, the values should be able to be considered equal.

From this, I think at the very least we could:

 - For primitive values, when comparing, ensure the encoding is DER and only then compare `.contents`
 - Define some comparison operators for other types that are well-defined, such as `x509.Certificate` and various structures in `algos` and `keys`

Hi amotl,
First, as peritus commented development of this project has been dead for a while. You should open this bug/feature request in bumpversion2.

As a workaround (and perhaps good practice) I would suggest you to use a version file. Basically create a "__version.py" file in module folder. The contents will be just the version:
```
__version__ = "0.0.1"
```
The, from your other files, import the version one and pick the version from that, e.g. setup.py:
```
...
setup(
    name='pymof',
    version=pymof.__version__,
   ...
```
Then your bumpersion.cfg only has to modify one file (the __version.py).

This makes bumpversion less prone to messing up and also makes it easier for manual housekeeping of versions (if needed at some point).

Yeah, `:` is technically legit on some platforms but can still cause problems, but I can see where we would want to avoid any chance of breaking functionality or backward compatibility. If you can think of some other way to get at the issue cleanly, I'd be all for it. Seems like we'd have to somehow define unique keys, since duplicate keys will overwrite each other and would not result in multiple bumpversion processing passes anyway. Perhaps something extra _before_ the filename, e.g.:
```
[bumpversion:file.0:myfile]
```
and corresponding re `^bumpversion:(file|part).*?:(.+)` ?

pip-review was not originally forked from pip, so merging the functionality into pip takes more than just a pull request. Currently, I'm not planning to spend any effort on that. If you do not want to depend on my whims, you have two options. The second option is to do the necessary work by yourself and submit a pull request to pip. Make sure to read their [contribution guidelines](https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/development/#pull-requests) in that case.

If you end up submitting a feature or pull request to the pip repository, please put a reference here so I can follow the progress. Thanks in advance.

OK, let's go through it.

If you do not specify the `--tmp-dir`, default a temporary directory will be created on the host, most probably as `/tmp/docker-squash-RANDOM_STRING`. This directory will be removed after squashing is finished.

Same applies to directory provided with the `--tmp-dir` parameter - this directory will be removed once squashing is finished.

In case you want to debug failure, you can leave the temporary directory for investigation, if you use the `-d` flag.

From what I understand you specified the `--tmp-dir` to an existing directory in which you had some files you cared about. Please note that name of this flag is "temporary directory" which implies that this is a short living directory. I would say that the only thing we should do is to extend the CLI `--help` page to mention that it will be removed after squashing is done.

OK I found the reason. In `MANIFEST.in`, the egg-info directory is ignored, and these files are necessary for easy_install to work. Buildout uses easy_install, thus triggers the error.
This can be easily reproduced with the command:
```
$ easy_install -md /tmp http://pypi.python.org/packages/source/p/python-social-auth/python-social-auth-0.2.10.tar.gz
```
The fix is as easy as:
```diff
diff --git a/MANIFEST.in b/MANIFEST.in
index 2cf5b48..d2fef43 100644
--- a/MANIFEST.in
+++ b/MANIFEST.in
@@ -7,7 +7,6 @@ recursive-include social/tests *.txt
 graft examples
 
 recursive-exclude .tox *
-recursive-exclude python_social_auth.egg-info *
 recursive-exclude social *.pyc
 recursive-exclude examples *.pyc
 recursive-exclude examples *.db
```

There are a couple possibilities. Thankfully, Mathworks adding a new HDF5 based MAT file format is not one of them https://www.mathworks.com/help/matlab/ref/save.html . R2016a doesn't have any format newer than v7.3.

1. A variable saved from Matlab is not compatible with the hdf5storage package. This would be any class. Sparses, while not supported yet, should at least be read as Numpy structured ndarrays. Matlab classes, when saved, have object references. If there is a circular set of references in how the class is stored, then hdf5storage will read them all repeatedly before running into a memory problem. It currently has no way to detect such a circular dependency (such testing should be added, though).
2. The MAT file has a variable that would be read as a structured Numpy ndarray but would have a dtype larger than signed 32-bits due to the sizes of all fields (each less than signed 32-bits) adding up to more than signed 32-bits (last I checked, size checking is only done on each field, not overflow checking on their sum).

Since it sometimes throws an error, the issue is probably not hdf5storage's poor speed performance.

Can you describe what the types and dimensions of the variables you are trying to read are? Also, are you able to read MAT files with a small number of simple variables (small double arrays, small int8 arrays, simple structures, simple cell arrays, etc.)?

I dug into this a bit and have a theory on why this is happening. If I'm correct, the code here:
https://github.com/typemytype/booleanOperations/blob/ebeaf6ba940dbfeda25b232a065f0cabf8ec9a8f/Lib/booleanOperations/flatten.py#L877

is trying to match an input curve (from the original glyph) with an output curve (from the union-ed polygon) by their first points. It searches for `t` values in the input curve for the last point on the output curve, and if it doesn't find one, as it might not in the example here, it just drops an entire segment because it assumes at least one would be found:
https://github.com/typemytype/booleanOperations/blob/ebeaf6ba940dbfeda25b232a065f0cabf8ec9a8f/Lib/booleanOperations/flatten.py#L924

I'm not sure if I'm right on the mark in diagnosing the actual issue, and I certainly don't know what the solution would be at this point. But maybe this is a start.

After some further investigation, it appears the phenomena is associated with calling the startup file a second time. Whenever a function references the same file that houses the app object for the first time, it re-initializes the startup jobs. So, since the startup job launch:app.logger.debug was referencing it's own file, it was duplicated along with all the other jobs added to the scheduler by commands in launch.py

This may be a feature of how Flask_APScheduler or APScheduler itself retrieves callables. But, it is definitely occurring both in my windows environment and my alpine linux virtualbox.

For most things, I think I can work around this problem by putting the callables in other files, but it may be tricky trying to incorporate the app object into other callables (for say logging) since any reference to the file with the scheduler seems to invoke it a second time.

Thoughts?

Here is useful link about this topic:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/9449101/how-to-stop-flask-from-initialising-twice-in-debug-mode

> The simplest thing to do here would be to add use_reloader=False to your call to app.run - that is: `app.run(debug=True, use_reloader=False)`
> 
> Alternatively, you can check for the value of WERKZEUG_RUN_MAIN in the environment:
> 
```
if os.environ.get("WERKZEUG_RUN_MAIN") == "true":
    # The reloader has already run - do what you want to do here
```
> However, the condition is a bit more convoluted when you want the behavior to happen any time except in the loading process:
> 
```
if not app.debug or os.environ.get("WERKZEUG_RUN_MAIN") == "true":
    # The app is not in debug mode or we are in the reloaded process
```

It looks like this was implemented sometime: there is currently code to filter out non-selectable items.

However, it has a bug. When browsing rather than searching, the pattern asks the server for all types, and then filters them on the client side. This is so that it can include folderish types in the results. But, if there are multiple pages of results and all of the results in the first page are not selectable, then the pattern shows a "no results" message even if there are valid results on later pages.

I suspect that the right solution is to configure the pattern with a list of folderish types, and make it ask the server for the union of folderish and selectable types when browsing, so that this filtering happens on the server side rather than the client side.

I have a few users who keep hitting the infinite redirect, so I don't think the theory that it was a transition issue holds water.

Maybe what's going on is that the browser goes to `/oidc/callback` with an error. The callback view has no explicit handling for this. The callback view redirects the user to the failure url which by default is `/`. Standup is using the middleware, so then the middleware processes that request and sends the browser back to Mozilla SSO which sends the browser back to `/oidc/callback` with the same error. Repeat.

That sure seems like what's going on.

Assuming that's true, I think to fix this we should:

1. add handling in `/oidc/callback` for when the querystring params have `error` and `error_description` and redirect to the failure url carrying that error code along
2. the failure url should not default to `/`--it should be an explicit view for "Yo--you fail at login." messages
3. the failure url should be exempted by the middleware

No, it won't. It is a common grievance, because you end up with numpy types inside plain Python structures.

Sometimes you don't notice until you try to serialize them and things blow up, such as when sending the objects through an HTTP interface. For example, json cannot serialize numpy types, and will throw an exception that will leave you scratching you head, unless you've seen it before :)

```python
>>> a = numpy.arange(10).astype(numpy.float32)
>>> l = list(a)
>>> json.dumps(l)
TypeError: 0.0 is not JSON serializable
```

This commit has broken a few of my S3CopyToTable tasks. In my tasks, the S3 path is dynamically determined at runtime, rather than passed in as a parameter to the job, so I need to override the `s3_load_path`, rather than define it as a class property.

The above case for which this PR was initially introduced could be fixed by something like this:

{code}
class MyRedshiftTask(S3CopyToTable):
    host = luigi.Parameter()
    database = luigi.Parameter()
    user = luigi.Parameter()
    password = luigi.Parameter()
    # ...

    load_path_param = luigi.Parameter()

    def s3_load_path(self):
        return self.load_path_param
{code}

If the S3CopyToTask is grabbing the `s3_load_path` value as a class property, then there's no way to define it dynamically.

That depends on how confident you are that your inputs are correct, and what your upgrade process looks like. If the data you send is invalid, the service will reject it, producing a `ClientError` on your side *after* you've already sent all the data. Many services have per-request pricing, so disabling local validation could potentially cost more than the saved cpu time. Dynamo gives free data in, though I don't know how invalid requests count against write capacity, if at all. I would guess that it won't count against you. Additionally, if there is a minor service update that changes parameter validation for Dynamo and you upgrade botocore without testing, you may start to see requests failing since you're using the updated model. This is not likely to happen, however.

In summary, here are the implications:

- You won't know about invalid data until after you've spent bandwidth sending it
- There will be a different class of errors when invalid data is found
- Depending on pricing model specifics, you could still be charged for invalid requests
- Updating botocore will require that you ensure your data still passes validation

@joguSD, Linux distributions are very much against vendor-specific versions of official packages like `requests`. I've tried to push a package using your vendored `requests` as a fix for #1258 for openSUSE, and it was rejected because of the security threats. I'm pretty certain other Linux distros have the same preferences. By shipping a custom `requests`, you essentially take over responsibility for security fixes for that package, which has a significantly larger attack surface than botocore itself. Is that something you want to carry around in the long term?

That said, I, and I guess most users, don't care whether you solve the issue by taking the patches from here or by just ripping out `requests`. That's fully up to you.

It could then be used in test data like this:

    ${cookie} =    Get Cookie    args    here
    Log    ${cookie.name}
    Should Be Equal    ${cookie.expiry}    ${expected}

2. Am I right that the expiry value means certain date? In that case it would be nice to be able to give it also in format like `2017-09-28 09:35:12`. There's `robot.utils.parse_time` that we could use here similarly as SL already uses `timestr_to_secs`. That `parse_time` also handles nice formats like `NOW` and `NOW + 1 day` out-of-the-box.

@jtara1 sorry you’re having an issue. All packaging downloads use wheel caches, and pipenv is no different. If you’ve been using pipenv to install your package, then you upload a new version to PyPI, you will have a cached version in the wheel cache. 

Pipenv doesn’t do any magic here, it only invokes pip to install packages. Nothing is at fault. I took a look at the warehouse and indeed your module is listed at version 0.1.3, and actually that is reflected in your installation (PyPI matches that lookup). 

However, I looked at your `setup.py` and it is using some different practices to determine version info which seem to be causing this issue, which is that the installed version tells the python distribution that it is version 0.1.0 while PyPI told you it gave you 0.1.3. This is from the root folder of your repository. Note that the `basename` of `dirname(abspath(setup.py))` is one level outside the repository so will never parse a version during install. 

```
__path = dirname(abspath(__file__))
# get module name from parent folder name
# assumes the parent folder (repository name) is the same as the module name
module_name = basename(__path)


def get_version_from_init():
    with open(join(__path, module_name, '__init__.py'), 'r') as init:
        match = re.search("__version__\s*=\s*'([\w.-]+)'", init.read())
    return match.group(1) if match is not None else None

# attempt to find variable module_name.__init__.__version__
__version__ = None
try:
    __version__ = get_version_from_init()
    if __version__ is None:
        print('[setup.py] Note: There was no __version__ variable within the '
              '__init__.py of the {}'.format(module_name))
    else:
        print('[setup.py] grabbed __version__ of {0} from {0}/__init__.py'
              .format(module_name))
except (FileExistsError, FileNotFoundError) as e:
    print(type(e), e)
    print('[setup.py] Note: There was no __init__.py found within {}'
          .format(module_name))


# -------------- Update the following variables --------------- #
# prioritize using __version__ in module_name.__init__ if it's there
version = '0.1.0' if __version__ is None else __version__
description = 'Python script/class to download an entire Imgur album in ' \
              ' one go into a folder of your choice'
# ------------------------------------------------------------- #
```

`test-import` is simply `import pkg_resources` in a text file. here, it takes about 200ms more starting Python3 with pkg_resources than without, a result consistent with others. The 345ms result above is probably due to the profiler overhead.. most of the time (217ms) is taken by `_initialize_master_working_set()`:

https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/5da3a845683ded446cad8af009d3ab9f264a944f/pkg_resources/__init__.py#L3193

Half of *that* time (104ms) is taken by `WorkingSet._build_master()`:

https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/5da3a845683ded446cad8af009d3ab9f264a944f/pkg_resources/__init__.py#L3205

If I would adventure a guess as to the other half, it's the `map` call a little later:

https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/5da3a845683ded446cad8af009d3ab9f264a944f/pkg_resources/__init__.py#L3228

`find_on_path` also seems like a pretty hot loop, called 500 times and doing pretty inefficient stuff like:

```python
    if os.path.isdir(path_item) and os.access(path_item, os.R_OK):
```

this is probably generating hundreds of `stat()` spurious syscalls. and that's just scratching the surface... there might be other nice optimization opportunities here... but really, there's a lot of work done here to list all packages. and i doubt we'll get to below 10-20ms (a 10-fold increase would be a nice first performance target) with all those...

isn't there a simpler way to do most of what we need here? for example, I just want the version number of *my* package: I don't want *all* version numbers of *all* packages or even the version number of arbitrary package *foo*. couldn't there be a cache built *on install* of the package metadata so *that* is available in a fast way? heck, the entrypoint stuff created by setup.py looks like this here:

@jaraco The bug happens trying to import `setuptools`/`pkg_resources` in an environment where a namespace package is installed, but where there is more than one directory in the path of the namespace package (e.g., one package in a "development install" vs. dependencies installed into `site-packages`).


In @jkbbwr's case above, what would cause `zope.__path__` to be an instance of `_NamespacePath` rather than the plain list that the code in `pkg_resources._rebuild_mod_path` clearly expects?  That class appears only to be instantiated by the stdlib's import machinery under Python 3.5+:

- `importlib._bootstrap_external._NamespaceLoader.__init__`
- `importlib._bootstrap_external.PathFinder.find_spec`.

As a hackaround, one might add monkeypatch a `sort` method onto `importlib._bootstrap_external._NamespacePath`. ;)

It's a good sign that the tests are passing, but I'm still a bit anxious because we didn't have a test for the `SandboxViolation` problem in the first place, I'm not how to tell if removing the `_ensure_directory_bypass` is going to cause a regression or not. I'm thinking it *is* going to cause a regression, because after quite a bit of digging around I can't see any substantial changes in behavior in the sandbox between now and when the original bypass commit occurred.

I think the best options for now are:

1. Try to write a failing test for the original `SandboxViolation` error so that we can see if we can fix it *without* writing our own `makedirs`.
2. Write a test that exercises something like @JonKohler's original patch and accept that. I'll note that [the upstream version of `makedirs` has a similar implementation](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Lib/os.py#L209).

I think the main problem here is that because `makedirs` is implemented in terms of `mkdir`, we need to re-implement `makedirs` in terms of the non-sandboxed `mkdir` in order to bypass the sandbox for `makedirs`. We may be able to change the implementation of the sandbox to make it easier to bypass the sandbox when using derived functions like `makedir` (for example, we could have the `os` wrapper add an `os._bypass_sandbox` submodule that exposes all the original functionality).

I agree with @jni, pertaining to the distinction between a rank-3 array intended to represent a 3D volume and a rank-3 array which represents channels or a collection of images with two spatial dimensions.

The ship has sailed and I realize why we opted to place z-axis in front of the other spatial axes for performance, but it remains unfortunate from an accessibility standpoint.  Users understand row, column, (plane) but when you lead with plane, it is significantly less clear.

As far as overall approach is concerned, consider we have multiple years of conference tutorials and other instruction materials which have been given surrounding the row/col convention.  Row/Col is massively beneficial for any users without a MATLAB background.  I do think we should retain r/c for consistency; thus, I actually favor the overall 'N_something' approach.  But, instead of capitalizing (thus emphasizing) N, what if we reversed it - the first line in the table above then becomes [nP, ]nR, nC.

Thanks for a quick response, Marcus @mrcslws 

> Questions:
    It sounds like you have a strong position against putting encoder Sensors next to their corresponding Encoders. Tentatively, I support the current approach. Otherwise we're polluting the codebase.

Not that strong :smiley: (as there's just 1 encoder now), but I don't see a reason for breaking the common pattern from python-nupic (which I take as standard) and even from current separation in nupic.core: `encoders/`, `regions/`, `algorithms/` - in my view the ScalarSensor is polluting the code-base; from the point of view of build-system: `encoders/` are stand-alone; `regions/` depend on other Regions and one on an encoder, now this made encoders depend on regions. All in all, it's 1:1 so unless there are better arguments I'd vote for moving in Reg.

>    Why should it implement encode? That's not a very C-like function.

I don't know, is it C++-like? :wink: Seriously, for these reasons: 
* python-Encoder defines `encode()` , not encodeIntoArray() which is just implementation detail (but used everywhere) - so API
* convenience and safety: as a user I'd really expect to feed an encoder a value and get bit-vector output. 
 - why would I have to allocate an array, know its type and size (I know it's common in C, but that does not mean the best), when I can get the correct data by return-value? Compare the knowledge required (and space for bugs) in: 
`auto result = e.encode(42);` vs 
```
vector<Real> oo(e.getOutputWidth());
e.encodeIntoArray(42, oo.data());
```
 - you might argue the speed, as `encode()` allocates a new array each call; but that's questionalble, as you have to clear your array somehow too in encodeIntoArray, just have to remember it. And it could be worked on, eg. EncoderBase could hold a member `private UInt output[size]` and we'd reuse it,... And still, `encodeIntoArray()` is there for those who need it. 

> What's wrong with Real64? It's like saying "double".

I think we have a switch whether `Real` should be interpreted as `Real32/Real64` (float/double), so if the code needs double, OK, otherwise why not leaving the more flexible?

> The network API uses floats (Real32). The _C++ encoders are just the internals of network API_ Sensors, refactored out of the Sensor. So the encoders should use floats, right?

This is one I feel strongly about. And I think we found the cause of different views: "C++ encoders are just the internals of network API" - No. In my POV the hierarchy from more-core to less-core nupic.core is: {{ {Encoders {SP/TP/TM}} Anomaly/Classifier} Regions/Network} Swarming...}

See #948 , maybe the The network API uses ~~floats (Real32)~~ could use UInts/or some flexible SDR representation object? 

In either case, SP+TM use `UInt[]` as I/O, so you can't avoid the Real->UInt change inside those regions, and having Encoder (Real) only helps you in 1 region but forces others to convert when they want to use it directly ( #889 ). 

> So far, I agree with:
    getOutputWidth could return a UInt.
    Templates might be useful.
        Though will it make SWIG bindings on the encoders complicated? I haven't really thought this through. 

I wouldn't know about the SWIG problems(?), but: other algos in core are templated too, who would use C++-wrappers for encoders? (when there is 1 enc in C++, and ALL in python; encoders are not the performance bottleneck; maybe for other languages?-do we care? )

    Parameter W = model.add_parameters({2, 2});

I like @perrygreenfield suggestion of having a function that can test if the coordinate is inside the domain or not. I find @nden suggestion _"...a model can be evaluated strictly over its domain (or bounding box) and the return values outside that should indicate that there's no solution there"_. I find this too restrictive and possibly imposing too much computational penalty. For example, imagine looking at an image in DS9 and then selecting a rectangular region that partly is outside the image [this is a quite common situation]. Currently DS9 can easily convert vertices of the polygon that are outside the image to sky coordinates. This is because the model gets extrapolated a little.

There is also another problem with domains: what do they mean/indicate? For example, let's take the beloved `wcs.WCS.all_world2pix()`. In some sense, here "domain" could mean the domain in which SIP correction was defined. This domain will likely be the entire chip/detector image: It is clear that distortion cannot be _measured_ outside the detector [at most, it could be computed _theoretically_ based on known optical properties of the system]. However, as with my DS9 example above, people customarily extrapolate the model outside its domain of definition. Then, there is another practical question: what is the _"domain"_ in which I can still invert SIP polynomials (even though I understand that outside the chip these polynomials do not describe distortion model very well)? Note: this is a different "domain" from the first (physical) "domain" described above which is used to describe the region in which distortion model was _measured_. 

Another example comes from using inverse SIP polynomials, kind of like what SPITZER did with their SIP from the beginning. My understanding is that this approach is favored by @nden [personally, I disagree with this approach for reasons I have outlined here:  https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/3675]. Then, a "domain" could mean the region within inverse SIP is within 1% (or 10%) from true value. So, this is a third interpretation of the world "domain". Using inverse SIP (``AP/BP_i_j``) outside the physical measurement domain is mathematically possible and it would be too restrictive not to allow users to do so if the wish.

Thus, the issue of "domains" is not trivial and imposing restrictions such as _"...a model can be evaluated strictly over its domain..."_ would limit the usability of the models.

the problem with the use of `==` is that it makes it impossible to install the afdko in the same environment alongside other tools that share some of the dependencies, e.g. fontmake.
I had to jump the hoops in order to be able to have both the latest fontmake (whose minimum requirements are greater than the latest afdko) and makeotf in the $PATH.
When using entry_points created via setuptools (as in `pip install -e .`), pkg_resources will check that the dependencies are satisfied:
```
pkg_resources.DistributionNotFound: The 'fonttools>=3.27.1' distribution was not found and is required by ufo2ft, fontmake
```
I understand the rationale for pinning in your case, since the afdko is really designed to be used as an "application" for end users, not as a library to import in other libraries/applications, so you would like the tools at each release to produce identical outputs and not change depending on the current versions of the dependencies.
But .. I have no but, really. I think it'd be better to use `~=` or `>=` in setup.py.

Yay, it is! Here's my snippet:
```
  Api:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Api
    Properties:
      StageName: Latest
      DefinitionBody:
        Fn::Transform:
          Name: AWS::Include
          Parameters:
            Location: !Sub s3://${ArtifactsBucket}/swagger.yaml
```
Then, in swagger.yaml I'm able to use `!Sub` and possibly other functions to reference resources, e.g.
```
x-amazon-apigateway-integration:
  type: aws_proxy
  httpMethod: GET
  uri:
    Fn::Sub: "arn:aws:apigateway:${AWS::Region}:lambda:path/2015-03-31/\
      functions/${GetApi.Arn}/invocations"
```
Obviously, I have to upload the swagger template to the specified bucket and pass the bucket name as a parameter to the SAM template prior to 'packaging' it. But it works! And allows me to preview the changeset, unlike with "nested" templates.

>Could you point to where that is happening? I don't see the change events that would result in every view being rerendered, or is it internal to Marionette? 

It's not internal to Mn. Mn 2.4.5 only implicitly re-renders items in a CollectionView. In all other cases the user is responsible for triggering a re-render. I do so here: https://github.com/MCGallaspy/kolibri/blob/um-modal/kolibri/core/assets/src/components_demo.js#L280

> If Marionette rerenders an entire collection view when only one of the models in it changes, I would think that is a fairly big black mark against Marionette.

I have not observed this to be the case. I haven't looked at the source, but it appears only items in a collection that are changed are re-rendered.

> My feeling about Virtual DOM or Incremental DOM integration is that it is something we should do before public release, but (as we may get reasonable performance without it) not absolutely require it for the MVP.

Maybe. With a larger app that contains many more DOM nodes then my aggressive re-rendering will get pokey. In contrast having each view manage when it or its child views are re-rendered is somehow conceptually simpler but a lot more verbose, and all that comes with verbosity. I think there are some trade offs to be made:

             | Aggressive re-rendering | View-managed re-rendering
----------|-----------------------------|-------------------------------
vDOM now |       High up-front dev time. IMO the ideal solution.           |    Most complexity. Probably not a good use case for vDOM, since view-managed re-rendering and vDOM overlap in the problems they're solving (i.e. selective re-rendering).
vDOM later |               Will likely encounter performance issues before vDOM integration. Integration later may require significant refactoring. Lowest up-front dev time, but sort of a gamble that performance will still be *good enough*.                |                    I'll interpret this as view-managed *for now* and then refactoring to use vDOM *later*. Still has a high up-front dev time, but *maybe* less than my ideal solution. This trades performance now with some complexity (i.e. larger surface area for bugs) for significant refactoring later and less complexity. In other words, taking on technical debt proportional to the # of front-end lines of code before we undertake the refactoring. IMO this approach is short sighted -- let's commit to one paradigm.


I'd rather commit to aggressive + vDOM integration now **or** (my 2nd choice) commit to view-managed re-rendering now and forget about vDOM integration at least for the foreseeable future.

> When running everything normal (including message broker on 8080), except for changing in these 2 lines 8080 to 9090 in client_aio.py:
> 
> url = os.environ.get('CBURL', u'ws://localhost:9090/ws')
> parser.add_argument('--url', dest='url', type=six.text_type, default=url, help='The router URL (default: "ws://localhost:9090/ws").')
> 
> Everything still works. This means that these values are ignored and a hard coded port is used.

I just tried and everything is working fine for me.

The first line reads the websocket url from environment variable `CBURL` and fallbacks to `ws://localhost:9090/ws`. So you probably have `CBURL` in the environment.

Just replace
`url = os.environ.get('CBURL', u'ws://localhost:9090/ws')`
with
`url = u'ws://localhost:9090/ws'`
and you should have no issue.

You can ignore the port number in second line, that's just a help comment and does not affect functionality.

a) What is the semantics when I provide a decorator with `options` _and_ I provide `options` to the `session.subscribe` later? Which one takes precedence? b) Recording the decorator infos as a list of tuples (within `_wampuris`): right now, we'd record pairs `(Pattern, RegisterOptions | SubscribeOptions)`. From an extensibility/maintainability perspective, maybe it would be better to put the Options things into the Patterns as an attribute:

```python
class Pattern(object):

        pass
```

This is the same root cause as #6809. The problem is that conda looks through its history file and sees that you've requested those specs in the past, so it adds them back.

In my opinion, the fix would either be for `conda env update` to not take specs from the history at all, or for `conda env` operations to not populate the history at all, or both.

If you'd like a workaround that is faster than `conda env create --force` you can truncate the history file located at `<env>/conda-meta/history` (the file needs to be present but can be empty).

@kalefranz, I'm looking at 4.0.x, and I see that `binstar_channel_alias` clobbers the value of `channel_alias` if `add_anaconda_token` is True. So with 4.0.x, it is not possible for them to be distinct. In other words, 4.0.x is assuming that _if_ `channel_alias` is set, that it points to the Anaconda Server instance. This means that @srossross's particular use case does not require a fix. I propose we leave it as is, then, and not try and backport a fix to #2556 and #1911.

```
res= es.search(index="test-index", doc_type='content-field',body={"query": {"match": {"text": {"query": "微观文明", "analyzer": "ik_smart"}}}})
```

I guess I should clarify some long-term goals for `pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks` itself first :)

In the further out long term, `pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks` should just contain language-agnostic hooks.  The initial (and current) implementation contains some python-specific hooks which I imagine will eventually find a home at something like (does not exist yet) `pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks-python`

Some effort has already been made in that direction: [mirrors-autopep8](https://github.com/pre-commit/mirrors-autopep8) has been separated obviating the need for `autopep8-wrapper` https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks/issues/92 -- autopep8-wrapper was originally written because https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/issues/285 was not implemented -- that is a hook could silently modify files (which should be staged for a commit) and would succeed.  Once pre-commit adjusted to make this a failure case, the usefulness of the wrapper diminished greatly.

In a lot of ways, `pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks` isn't far off from needing a split.  It has a bit of a sprawling-dependency problem and I don't really want to make that worse by adding yet-another dependency (when it isn't necessarily going to be used _most_ of the time).  Logically, I think `pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks` could have the following hooks split out to a python-specific repository _today_ and start the migration: `autopep8-wrapper` (though, probably deprecate), `check-ast`, `check-builtin-literals`, `check-docstring-first`, `debug-statements`, `double-quote-string-fixer`, `fix-encoding-pragma`, `flake8` + `pyflakes` (probably move to a mirror, or attempt to upstream the configuration), `name-tests-test`, `requirements-txt-fixer`.

Now onto `reorder_python_imports` vs `pre-commit-python-sorter`

I definitely agree that import sorting is by far one of the most important hooks for python repositories.  A lot of the motivation for using a framework like this is to get high quality fixers and use them!

`reorder_python_imports` (at the moment) largely supports a *very specific* style of imports -- the style that _I_ prefer and isn't very configurable (whether you consider this a plus or minus is up to interpretation 😆).  There is one core benefit that it currently has over `isort` though I think these could be fixed on `isort`'s side -- though I've tried in the past and didn't get far: https://github.com/timothycrosley/isort/issues/184.  This is that `isort` is pretty poor at classifying imports -- mostly relying on manual configuration and [a crazy long hardcoded list](https://github.com/timothycrosley/isort/blob/3b011412742d09ba768346379a52cd0784d1514f/isort/settings.py#L57-L103).  Here, `reorder_python_imports` (well, more accurately [`aspy.refactor_imports`](https://github.com/asottile/aspy.refactor_imports) the actual classifying implementation) excels by intelligently classifying imports based on project structure.

The other main difference (and in my mind a _minor_ difference) is `reorder_python_imports` is _much_ more timid about reordering imports when there is interspersed code.  I chose this design as I wanted import reordering to be as failproof as possible -- now there _are_ situations where imports *must* be ordered in a specific way and they'd still break under both implementations -- just `reorder_python_imports` is significantly more conservative.

On to `pre-commit-python-sorter`!  As far as I can tell from reading the source, this is just a simple wrapper around `isort`'s library functionality.  I _think_ (and may be incorrect in this assertion!) that it was written before (1) `isort` could be easily used directly (2) pre-commit would consider modifications a failure.   This necessitated a trivial wrapper which added this functionality -- and `pre-commit-python-sorter` was born!  As far as I can tell, `mirrors-isort` (or metadata added to `isort` itself) should completely obsolete this.

Your question made to realise that I may have asked the question to the wrong place. I've just searched for resumption in https://docs.python.org/3.6/library/ssl.html, and I found nothing. I guess I should open a ticket to python to add support for TLS session resumption with tickets.

TLS session resumption saves one SSL session handshake after the first SSL session. If you have RTT of 100ms between client and servers, then you will spend 200ms just to establish an SSL session to the server as an SSL session requires two handshakes. You can skip the second SSL handshake by resuming a previously established SSL session. There are two ways to use TLS session resumption, either with Session IDs or Tickets, the latter is the prefered way these days as it doesn't require to store on the server side a session for each client.

My guess would be the iframe in the `/view/` handler. When you request `/view/n/img/Energy-density.jpg`, the response is a small HTML page containing a sandboxed iframe, within which the browser will make a new request for `/files/n/img/Energy-density.jpg` (which serves the actual image). The sandboxing means that the browser treats the iframe itself as a separate domain, so we get cross-origin protections. That's important because otherwise viewing an HTML file would let it execute code through a kernel. But I bet it also means that the browser sees the cookies it's sending to the notebook server as 'third party' cookies, because they're not tied to the sandbox domain.

In principle, we only need the sandboxing for file types that can run JS in the browser, like HTML. So we could reduce the impact by not using the iframe for images. But I don't know exactly what that set is - can SVG run JS? Are there other HTML-like types that the browser might display? And I don't see how we can avoid the third-party cookie issues for viewing HTML files.

I just happened to notice that now `\\(` is supported in the notebook as math delimiter, and I think this is a really bad idea!

This goes very much against the spirit of Markdown and is very inconsistent from a Markdown standpoint. A backslash is normally used to avoid something being interpreted as formatting command, here it is used for the exact opposite!

It also increases the already bad fragmentation of Markdown extensions.

What about just not supporting this style of separators?
We have already `$` and `$$` and `\begin{equation}...\end{equation}` (and many other math environments) ... why do we need more?

The distinction between "expected Jupyter behavior" (leading to a format spec change) and "supported by a few tools" (could be implemented by a conventional tag) can be confusing when one official reference frontend is by far most popular and implements a specific convention. I think we want to avoid "the implementation is the standard" - but of course it can be difficult to set that expectation about what is implementation and what is the standard.

@jmsdnns, @kstrauser, any thoughts on the proposed patch #368?

To summarize, it's a variant of [this idea](https://github.com/schematics/schematics/issues/324#issuecomment-155924477) that already got tentative support, but instead of using `to_primitive` and `to_native` as the universal interface I landed on `export` and `convert`.

The reason is that the *format* (primitive/native) is just a detail that is subordinate to the *process* (import/export). Compound types don't care about the format at all. It's also more flexible this way, 'cause now the formats aren't hardcoded in the interface. A custom type can implement a third format if it wants.

This is what `ListType.export` (formerly `export_loop`) looks like now:

```python
def export(self, list_instance, format, context):
    data = []
    for value in list_instance:
        # Just call `export` on the field; no more inspecting for `export_loop`.
        shaped = self.field.export(value, format, context)
        # Filter out empty values as before...
        ...elif shaped is not None:
            data.append(shaped)
    return data
```

When the process reaches the atomic field, `BaseType.export()` will call either `self.to_primitive()` or `self.to_native()` based on the format.

There are "only" two breaking changes:

* Custom compound types must implement the new interface by turning `to_native` into `convert` and `export_loop` into `export`, but this is very straightforward.

* `Model.to_native()` now actually returns a native value (instance) instead of a dict.

From that perspective `convert` could become a private method `_convert`.

			print 'commiting'

			break
finally:
	print 'closing'
	c.close()
	print 'closed'

That said, when adding `fromisoformat` to CPython (which *does* have support for sub-minute and sub-second offsets), because both the naive portion of the time and the offset portion of the time ended up having the exact same format requirements (e.g. `HH[:MM[:SS[.fff[fff]]]]`), I just [wrote a single parse function that is used for both of them](https://github.com/python/cpython/commit/09dc2f508c8513e0466a759cc27a09108c1e55c2#diff-25e2d173c84057d069b7890450714eddR314). The problem seems to be with in the `automodule` directive of [`docs/tz.rst`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dateutil/dateutil/master/docs/tz.rst). I'm not sure why exactly, but it seems that `gettz` is not being considered a "member" even though it is in `dateutil.tz.__all__`. If you explicitly add it:

```rst
==
tz
==
.. automodule:: dateutil.tz
   :members:
   :undoc-members:

   .. automethod:: dateutil.tz.gettz
```

It will pick up the docstring from `GettzFunc`.

That said, other than manually writing docs for the full public API of `gettz` into `GettzFunc`, I don't know if there's an easy way to include the members of the `gettz` object, like `nocache` and `clear_cache`. We can figure that out later.

I don't know why, but this bug reveals that Cython ``cpdef`` methods could not be overridden by Python classes, at least in current case.

As an interim solution, I have updated example below to just override ``__call__`` method and added ``Provider.last_overriding`` read-only property:

```python
"""Custom `Factory` example."""

import dependency_injector.providers as providers


class User(object):
    """Example class User."""


class UsersFactory(providers.Provider):
    """Example users factory."""

    __slots__ = ('_factory',)

    def __init__(self):
        """Initializer."""
        self._factory = providers.Factory(User)
        super(UsersFactory, self).__init__()

    def __call__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        """Return provided object.

        Callable interface implementation.
        """
        if self.last_overriding is not None:
            return self.last_overriding._provide(args, kwargs)
        return self._factory(*args, **kwargs)


# Users factory:
users_factory = UsersFactory()

# Creating several User objects:
user1 = users_factory()
user2 = users_factory()

# Making some asserts:
assert isinstance(user1, User)
assert isinstance(user2, User)
assert user1 is not user2
```

It's the best way I found for removing this confusion.

This patch has been already productized under version ``3.4.4``: https://pypi.python.org/pypi/dependency_injector/3.4.4

Hello,

yes, for the hash functions which depend on memory location, I don't see a solution. It seems that CVC4 and Alt-ergo don't do such things.

Considering the "caching" of VCs you describe - we have such a mechanism, but it can't always be used in nightly testing. There are two different use cases: we as a tool vendor, and the customer as a user of the tool.

As a tool vendor, we want to make sure that the tool works well without any kind of cache, and we also want to catch potential problems e.g. in the SMT solvers. So *not* running the SMT solver in the nightly run (because of using the cache instead) would partly defeat the purpose of nightly testing.

Most users of SPARK are in a context where some kind of certification is required. Some customer explained this problem to us, unfortunately I forgot half of the explanation. What I recall is that a VC cache is bad, because the cache is both an input and an output of the verification process, which is something the certification standards don't like. So they definitely can't use the cache for the "final", validating run of the tool, and they want to make sure regularly that they can do this run at any time, so in the end they will not use the cache in nightly testing, only as a convenience in day-to-day development.

A last thing, the main purpose of steps is not to reproduce *proofs* reliably, but reproduce *any* tool output reliably, whether that is UNSAT or UNKOWN or something else. When I run "cvc4 --rlimit=1000 some_vc", I will get always the same result, whether cvc4 manages to prove the VC or not.

I'm not sure I convinced you of the usefulness of the steps limit, what I can assure you that if Z3 has such an option, we will use it :-)

On face value that seems like a nice convenience for the developer (and I did that in fs1), but there are so many pitfalls with `__del__`. FS objects are much more complex beasts than file objects, and may have all sorts of references to other objects. Which means that you can't be certain what order they will be closed in. Consequently you can get bugs (or warnings) that appear only occasionally from one run to the next; tests are harder; things can behave differently depending on the scope you created the FS object; and the there are major differences across Python implementations (pypy in particular). 

Which is why in fs2 I was reluctant to use them. Even the mere presence of a `__del__` method can change the behaviour of the garbage collector.

If a context manager isn't appropriate, then I'd say that an app should define some kind of shut-down hook to close any filesystems. Most frameworks, gui or web, will provide some way of doing that. Alternatively there's always the weakref or atexit modules.

I could be persuaded otherwise, but I strongly suspect `FS.__del__` would be more trouble than its worth...

I'll answer my own question here- this is neither necessary nor (probably) idiomatic Python.

What we do instead is define our own django Model classes with `managed = False` in the metaclass, then we simply parse over records / lists of our abstract `Agent` type before returning from resolvers and construct new objects of the derived `Person` / `Organization` types by passing attributes from the returned `Agent` records in as parameters.

The relevant code looks like this:

```
# helper method

def formatAgent(agent):
    if agent.agent_type.party_type == "individual":
        return Person(
            id=agent.id,
            name = agent.name,
            note = agent.description,
            image = agent.image)
    else:
        return Organization(
            id=agent.id,
            name = agent.name,
            note = agent.description,
            image = agent.image,
            is_context = agent.is_context,
            type = agent.agent_type)

# use in query resolvers:

    def resolve_my_agent(self, args, *rargs):
        agent = self._load_own_agent()
        if agent:
            return formatAgent(agent)
        raise PermissionDenied("Cannot find requested agent")
```

```
import numpy as np
import scipy as sp

from sklearn.utils.multiclass import unique_labels
from sklearn.utils.fixes import bincount
from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelEncoder
from sklearn.metrics.classification import _prf_divide

from unittest import TestCase


def g_mean(y_true, y_pred, labels=None, correction=0.001):
    present_labels = unique_labels(y_true, y_pred)

    if labels is None:
        labels = present_labels
        n_labels = None
    else:
        n_labels = len(labels)
        labels = np.hstack([labels, np.setdiff1d(present_labels, labels,assume_unique=True)])

    le = LabelEncoder()
    le.fit(labels)
    y_true = le.transform(y_true)
    y_pred = le.transform(y_pred)
    sorted_labels = le.classes_

    # labels are now from 0 to len(labels) - 1 -> use bincount
    tp = y_true == y_pred
    tp_bins = y_true[tp]

    if len(tp_bins):
        tp_sum = bincount(tp_bins, weights=None, minlength=len(labels))
    else:
        # Pathological case
        true_sum = tp_sum = np.zeros(len(labels))

    if len(y_true):
        true_sum = bincount(y_true, weights=None, minlength=len(labels))

    # Retain only selected labels
    indices = np.searchsorted(sorted_labels, labels[:n_labels])
    tp_sum = tp_sum[indices]
    true_sum = true_sum[indices]

    recall = _prf_divide(tp_sum, true_sum, "recall", "true", None, "recall")
    recall[recall == 0] = correction

    return sp.stats.mstats.gmean(recall)


class TestEvaluator(TestCase):
    def test_g_mean(self):
        cor = 0.001
        y_true = [0, 0, 0, 0]
        y_pred = [0, 0, 0, 0]
        self.assertAlmostEqual(g_mean(y_true, y_pred, correction=cor), 1.0, places=10)

        y_true = [0, 0, 0, 0]
        y_pred = [1, 1, 1, 1]
        self.assertAlmostEqual(g_mean(y_true, y_pred, correction=cor), cor, places=10)

        y_true = [0, 0, 1, 1]
        y_pred = [0, 1, 1, 0]
        self.assertAlmostEqual(g_mean(y_true, y_pred, correction=cor), 0.5, places=10)

        y_true = [0, 1, 2, 0, 1, 2]
        y_pred = [0, 2, 1, 0, 0, 1]
        self.assertAlmostEqual(g_mean(y_true, y_pred, correction=cor), (1*cor*cor)**0.33, places=10)

        y_true = [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
        y_pred = [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
        self.assertAlmostEqual(g_mean(y_true, y_pred, correction=cor), 1, places=10)

        y_true = [0, 1, 1, 1, 1, 0]
        y_pred = [0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 1]
        self.assertAlmostEqual(g_mean(y_true, y_pred, correction=cor), (0.5*0.75)**0.5, places=10)
```

I was just watching a C++Now talk by Marshall Clow and he happened to mention this very issue: https://youtu.be/U3jGdnRL3KI?t=33m40s -- link is timestamped to start at the relevant part and it lasts about a minute and a half. (The entire talk is also great.)

TL;DW Taking the address of standard library function is not portable. Implementations are allowed to have an overload set where you would not expect one or mark a function `always_inline`. The only portable way to get a pointer is to wrap the `std` function in a lambda.

We ran into quite some problems with the "arithmethics" of the RTO acquistion mode not working correctly if a single run has not been completed. The snippet below implements checking for progress and should resolve this issue. 

I think this should be included in the `.trace()` method that acquires a trace with potentially an option to disable this if fast acquisiton is required. 

```python 
    rto.ch2.arithmetics('AVERAGE')  # other options: 'ENVELOPE' and 'OFF'
    rto.ch2.trace.prepare_trace()
    rto.run_single()  # and perform a single run.

    # This snippet should be included in the get_trace method
    t0 = time.time()
    timeout = 60
    num_avg = int(rto.ask('ACQ:COUNT?'))
    curr_acq =0
    while curr_acq < num_avg:
        curr_acq = int(rto.ask('ACQ:CURR?'))
        t1 = time.time()
        dt = t1-t0
        # prints the progress in a friendly manner :)
        print('\rAt {}/{} averages, acquiring for {:.2f}s'.format(
            curr_acq, num_avg, dt), end='')
        if dt> timeout:
            raise TimeoutError('More than {}s expired'.format(timeout))

    y = rto.ch2.trace()
```

Hmm, here's another trick, but it might not be widely applicable enough to be worthwhile: the `send` and `recv` syscalls accept a `flags` argument, and one of the flags you can pass is `MSG_DONTWAIT`, which makes a socket effectively non-blocking *just for this call*, without affecting any global state.

But... AFAICT this is supported only on Linux, not Windows or MacOS. On MacOS, the `MSG_DONTWAIT` constant appears to be defined, but it's not mentioned in the `send` man page, and it doesn't seem to work:
```python
# MacOS
In [1]: import socket

In [2]: socket.MSG_DONTWAIT
Out[2]: 128

In [3]: a, b = socket.socketpair()

In [4]: while True:
   ...:     print("sending")
   ...:     res = a.send(b"x" * 2 ** 16, socket.MSG_DONTWAIT)
   ...:     print("sent", res)
   ...:     
sending
[...freezes...]
```
And on Windows it doesn't appear to be either documented or defined.

And, even on Linux, it only works on sockets. If I try using nasty tricks to call `send` on a pty, I get:
```python
# Linux
In [3]: s = socket.fromfd(1, socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)

In [4]: s.send(b"x")
OSError: [Errno 88] Socket operation on non-socket
```
and similarly on a pipe:
```python
# Linux
In [5]: p1, p2 = os.pipe()

In [6]: s = socket.fromfd(p2, socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)

In [7]: s.send(b"x")
OSError: [Errno 88] Socket operation on non-socket
```

This has me wondering though if there's any other way to get a similar effect. There was a Linux patch [submitted in 2007](https://lwn.net/Articles/216443/) to make Linux native AIO work on pipes and sockets; I don't know if it was merged, but in principle it might be usable to accomplish a similar effect.

On pipes, if no-one else is reading from the pipe, then the `FIONREAD` `ioctl` can be used to find out how many bytes are ready to be read, so reading that much won't block. Of course, someone else might be reading from the pipe at the same time, steal them out from under you, and then you get blocked for an arbitrary amount of time, whoops. And for writing, there doesn't seem to be any similar trick (you can use `F_GETPIPE_SZ` to find out how big the pipe buffer is, but not how full it is; possibly there's some undocumented IOCTL somewhere that I'm missing). So maybe this is useless.

Maybe we should focus on making threaded I/O as fast as possible :-)

---------

Unrelated issue: there's also some question about how a hypothetical `trio.stdout` should respond if someone replaces `sys.stdout`. This is a fairly common and supported thing. If we just do `trio.stdout = trio.wrap_file(sys.stdout)`, and then someone does `sys.stdout = ...`, then `trio.stdout` will keep pointing to the old `stdout`. OTOH, if we make `trio.stdout` a special object that always looks up `sys.stdout` on every call, then... it won't work, because of locking issues. Le sigh.

I think having the span references as tags/links would be a good start. Another part that would be useful is: when showing a trace, also show any spans that reference spans in that trace.

I'm not sure exactly how this second part would be implemented in the query layer. When retrieving a given trace, we'd need to find any spans that reference that trace, plus all of their descendants. I assume that one of the spans in the set of parent references would have to be chosen as the "primary", from which we'd assign the trace id. So, maybe (1) find all spans with references to the trace, that aren't already part of the trace (i.e., that don't have the same trace id) (2) get the trace ids for those spans, (3) fetch those traces, (4) post filter for spans that are descendants of the referencing spans.

This assumes we have a way to perform query (1) above.

It almost seems like it'd be easier to create a new trace id if you have multiple parents. In this way it could be modeled as a sub-trace that belongs to multiple other traces, defined via references. This would eliminate the need to post-filter, and would get rid of the arbitrary selection of a primary span. (Also what if the "primary" span is not sampled but some other parent is? - in this solution we'd sample the new trace if any parent is samped)

There's also a question of whether other parent references of those spans should be included in the view - but for the use case I'm describing here, that wouldn't be necessary/useful. I'd rather click a link to view traces for events in the same batch.

These should be fixed versions of the V1 files. Namely convert-v1 and convertv1t both files should work. They should be totally displayed rather than simply displayed by a single dot.

The error with the single dot was due to what turned out to python v2 v3 error where division was changed from integer division to floating point native. Which means if I didn't specify the floating point division for the v2 it caused an integer division that became 0 rather than something like 0.2948... which caused the scaling on the image render everything in the graphic into the centermost pixel. That seems to be the bug with reference to the pec graphic not displaying. These 2 files should prove that fact.

[pesv1fixed.zip](https://github.com/EmbroidePy/pyembroidery/files/2308807/pesv1fixed.zip)

```
    scale_x = (graphic_width - buffer) / float(diagram_width)
    scale_y = (graphic_height - buffer) / float(diagram_height)
```

That would suffice for the most part as a fix.
